title-group.article-title: Renal candidiasis associated with papillary necrosis in a captive tiger (Panthera tigris)

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceFloyd D. Wilson, Mississippi Veterinary Research & Diagnostic Laboratory, 612 Saw Pine Lane, Ridgeland, MS 39157, USA. Email: floydw8504.fw@gmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Authors. Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: A 14‐year‐old male tiger developed anorexia with elevated blood urea nitrogen and creatinine levels. The patient had a palpable abdominal mass and demonstrated neutrophilic leukocytosis and anaemia. Leukocytes, yeast and bacteria were present in the urine. The animal was non‐responsive to therapy and was subsequently euthanised. Extensive acute renal papillary necrosis (RPN) with pyelonephritis, chronic nephritis and polycystic renal disease were evident during gross and microscopic pathology examinations. The histologic occurrence of fungal spores and pseudohyphae morphologically consistent with Candida species were observed within the necrotic papillary regions of the kidney and within multiple foci of mild parakeratotic hyperkeratosis present in the gingiva and tongue. Candida albicans along with a slight growth of Escherichia coli were recovered from kidney cultures. Possible contributory factors for the renal candidiasis and associated RPN include predisposing oral candidiasis, polycystic renal disease, ischaemic nephrosclerosis, age‐associated or other forms of immunodeficiency and therapy with meloxicam, a non‐steroidal anti‐inflammatory drug. The absence of apparent lower urinary tract involvement coupled with the presence of intravascular renal ‘Candida emboli’ suggest that chronic oral candidiasis was the probable source of the kidney infection.

The clinicopathological findings for a 14‐year‐old male tiger with anorexia and elevated blood urea nitrogen and creatinine levels are described. The tiger was non‐responsive to therapy and euthanised. Acute renal papillary necrosis (RPN) with pyelonephritis, chronic nephritis producing nephrosclerosis and polycystic renal disease were evident during gross and microscopic pathologic examinations. Fungal spores and pseudohyphae consistent with Candida species were observed within the necrotic papillary regions of the kidney and also within foci of parakeratotic hyperkeratosis present on the gingiva and tongue. Candida albicans was recovered from the kidney. Possible contributory factors for the renal disease include predisposing oral candidiasis, polycystic renal disease, ischaemic nephrosclerosis, age, immunodeficiency and meloxicam therapy. The absence of apparent lower urinary tract involvement coupled with the presence of chronic oral candidiasis and renal ‘Candida emboli’ suggest a probable haematogenous pathogenesis for the kidney infection.

p.mixed-citation: Wilson, F. D., Baughman, B., Mauel, M. J., Holifield, M., Todd, D., & Pace, L. (2024). Renal candidiasis associated with papillary necrosis in a captive tiger (Panthera tigris). Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1421. 10.1002/vms3.142138779883PMC11112537

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: There are few reports in the scientific literature addressing the occurrence of renal disease in exotic felines including tigers (Panthera tigris) (McCullagh & Lucke, 1978; Newkirk et al., 2011; Wolf et al., 1991), and to our knowledge renal candidiasis associated with renal papillary necrosis (RPN) has not been described for this species. This report documents the development of renal candidiasis associated with RPN occurring in an aged male tiger manifesting with terminal uremia. Chronic fibrosing nephritis, polycystic renal disease and mild multifocal gingival and glossal parakeratotic hyperkeratosis with intra‐lesional Candida were also present.

sec.title: METHODS
sec.p: Soft tissue samples were collected from all major organs including those exhibiting gross pathology. Following 10% neutral formalin fixation and routine histological processing, 5 µm sections were prepared and stained with haematoxylin and eosin. Select tissues were also stained with the Gomori's methenamine silver and the periodic acid–Schiff methods to demonstrate fungal elements.

Microscopic evaluations were performed using an Olympus CX31 microscope (Olympus Corporation). Photomicrographs were taken with an AmScope MU1403 camera (AmScope) using the associated software‐based program.

Routine bacterial and fungal cultures were performed on the kidney sample using Sabouraud dextrose agar, 5% sheep blood agar, MacConkey (MAC) agar, chocolate agar, Columbia nalidixic acid agar (CAN) and Thioglycollate/brain heart infusion broth. A moderate growth of Candida albicans was found on the CAN plates and the identity confirmed by Rapid Yeast plus (Remel, Thermo Fisher Scientific). A slight growth of Escherichia coli was noted on the MAC agar plates as identified by analysis with the Sensititre ARIS 2X system (TREK Diagnostic Systems, Inc.).

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: This 14‐year‐old male Sumatran tiger was a resident of the Jackson Zoo andits clinical history included a healed comminuted right tibia–fibula fracture and broken canine teeth. Upon arrival at Jackson, its weight was 111.1 kg, and the blood urea nitrogen (BUN) was 60 mg/dL (normal range 6.5–48.2 mg/dL). After arrival, it fell on its left shoulder, subsequently was limping on its left front leg and was treated with meloxicam 7.5 mg daily for 3 days. Because of a decreased appetite, it received injections of dexamethasone and Baytril (enrofloxacin) for several days until its appetite improved. It also received four 960 mg sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim tablets and two 20 mg prednisone tablets daily for about 6 days until it refused medication.

To perform a detailed clinical examination, it was sedated with a combination cocktail composed of 4 mg/kg ketamine plus 2 mg/kg xylazine antagonised with 0.125 mg/kg yohimbine. The examination revealed that the upper left canine needed removal. An abdominal mass was palpated during physical examination. Cytology performed on a needle aspirate from the mass was non‐diagnostic demonstrating only possible sampling‐associated haemorrhage. The urine sediment at this time demonstrated 25–50 leukocytes per microscopic field with moderate numbers of yeast and bacteria observed. The peripheral white blood cell (WBC) count at that examination time was 16,600 per microliter, the blood differential revealed 98% neutrophil and the haematocrit was 28.8%. The BUN now was 193 mg/dL and the creatinine 13.2 mg/dL (normal range 1.6–4.6 mg/dL). One week later the WBC was elevated to 20.4K/µL with 92% neutrophils, the haematocrit had fallen to 13.5%, the BUN was 201 mg/dL and the creatinine 12.0 mg/dL. Ten days prior to termination, the tiger developed anorexia, and due to the associated elevated BUN and creatine, it likely reflected uremia. It was non‐responsive to therapy and following sedations with a ketamine/xylazine combination, it was euthanised using euthasol euthanasia solution and prescribed procedures (AVMA Guidelines for the Euthanasia of Animals, 2013). The terminal body weight was 91.2 kg.

Gross pathology examination demonstrated that the body appeared thin with evidence of atrophy of the muscles of the rear legs (Figure 1a). There were fractures of the left upper and lower canine teeth, generalised gingival thickening was present and the oral mucosa appeared pale (Figure 1b).

caption.p: Gross pathology findings. (a) Extensive atrophy of the rear leg musculature is apparent. (b) Oral cavity. Broken teeth along with pallor and thickening of the oral mucosa is apparent. (c) Multifocal haemorrhage of the gastric mucosa that is consistent with uremia is present. (d) Multinodular mass within the mesenteric is present that microscopically was consistent with a lipoma. (e) Kidney demonstrating pale regions of renal papillary necrosis (black arrow). Contrast this region with the more normal medullary area (white arrow). (f) Kidney area showing multiple empty cystic structures.

sec.p: The right proximal tibia–fibula region was enlarged and contained a smooth‐surfaced, rounded and firm bony callus. The lungs were pale, swollen and wet. Pressure resulted in expression of large amounts of fluid from the cut lung surfaces. The lymph nodes generally were small. The stomach was filled with abundant dark green–black soft ingesta and extensive irregular foci of gastric mucosal haemorrhage were seen (Figure 1c). The small intestines contained moderate to abundant reddish to dark brown mucoid material and the colon abundant very dark brown–red soft faeces. An approximately 7 × 5 × 2 cm irregular bright yellow–orange rubbery multinodular ‘cauliflower‐like’ mass was present in the central mesentery (Figure 1d).

The kidneys had grey–white and slightly irregular capsular surfaces and the cortices on cut‐surface appeared pale brown having a firm rubbery consistency (Figure 1e). The generally tan medullary papillary regions exhibited extensive irregular pale yellow foci with dark red mottling. Several, 0.3–2.5 cm in diameter, discrete, rounded and variably multiloculated cystic structures containing clear fluid were present in both kidneys (Figure 1f).

The major gross pathology diagnoses were chronic nephropathy, polycystic kidneys, chronic gingivitis, fractured canine teeth, mesenteric mass (possible fat necrosis), right proximal tibia–fibula bone fracture callus, pulmonary congestion with oedema and, bloody‐appearing gastrointestinal contents (suggesting possible uremia). At the time of necropsy, a sample of kidney was submitted for bacteriology.

Histologic examination demonstrated marked advanced chronic active renal pathology (Figure 2a,b). There was variable yet diffuse atrophy of the cortical tubular epithelium associated with mild to marked interstitial fibrosis. The cortical tubules often appeared dilated and lined with attenuated epithelium. More pronounced irregular segmental regions of fibrosis with marked tubular and glomerular atrophy were sometimes present radiating through the cortex. Similar atrophy and dilation of the medullary collective ducts were appreciated in association with interstitial fibrosis. Mild to moderate multifocal interstitial lymphoplasmacytic inflammatory cell infiltration was evident in the cortex and in regions of the pelvic connective tissues. The glomeruli often appeared either shrunken or slightly enlarged. Minimal to mild thickening of the glomerular basement membranes was sometimes present as was glomerulosclerosis, and fibrotic crescents within Bowman's capsule.

caption.p: Histological findings. (a) Kidney demonstrating chronic nephropathy. There is renal cortical tubular dilation with attenuation of tubular epithelium that is associated with interstitial fibrosis. Mild multifocal mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltration and thickening of Bowman's capsular and sometimes glomerular basement membranes are also appreciated. Periodic acid–Schiff stain at 400× magnification. (b) Extensive renal papillary necrosis is evidenced in the left region that is associated with a proximal zone of marked and mainly neutrophilic inflammatory cell infiltration with neutrophil masses present within the necrotic collecting ducts. Haematoxylin and eosin stain at 400× magnification. (c) Higher magnification of region of papillary necrosis demonstrating large numbers of spores and pseudohyphae having morphology consistent with Candida species, Grocott's methenamine silver (GMS) stain at 1000× magnification. (d) Section of tongue demonstration masses of keratin intermixed with fungal elements located within the recesses between the glossal filiform papilla and foci of mild parakeratotic hyperkeratosis with intra‐lesional fungi present along the tip of one papilla, GMS stain at 400× magnification. (e) Higher magnification of the glossal papilla showing focus of parakeratotic hyperkeratosis and intra‐lesional Candida, GMS stain at 1000× magnification. (f) Small renal artery showing a micro‐thrombus containing fungal elements ‘fungal micro‐embolus’, GMS stain at 1000× magnification.

sec.p: Severe acute microscopic pathology was present in the renal medullary papillary regions, which demonstrated extensive necrosis progressing to liquefaction (Figure 2b). The papillary necrosis was associated with a proximal broad zone of marked neutrophilic inflammatory cell infiltration. Clusters of neutrophils and necrotic debris were also present within the lumen of the associated collecting ducts. Regions of the renal pelvis connective tissues exhibited multifocal neutrophilic or lymphocytic inflammatory cell infiltration. Acute micro‐thrombi were sometimes evident near the necrotic junction. In some cortical regions, large empty or fluid‐filled cystic structures were seen that were lined with simple flattened cuboidal epithelium. Mild multifocal mineralisation was occasionally present as were infrequent oxalate‐like crystals within cortical tubules.

Present within the necrotic tips of the involved renal papilla were large numbers of uniform septate fungal pseudohyphae (or hyphae) that often were giving rise to budding yeast forms. The yeast had morphology compatible with Candida species (Figure 2c) having an estimated width of 3–5 um. The mycotic organisms were particularly evident with Grocott's methenamine silver (GMS) staining (Figure 2c) and sporadic yeast was occasionally appreciated within the lumens of more proximal collecting ducts as well as within small renal vessels (Figure 2f). The renal histological diagnoses were acute necrotising pyelitis (RPN) with intra‐lesional fungal pseudohyphae and spores, chronic fibrosing interstitial nephritis (or pyelonephritis with nephrosclerosis) and polycystic renal disease.

Microscopically, multifocal regions of minimal mucosal epithelial hyperplasia associated with parakeratosis (parakeratotic hyperkeratosis) (Figure 2d,e) were present within the gingiva and along the surface of the tongue. Present within the microscopic hyperkeratotic plaques were seen mycotic pseudohyphae and spore forms compatible with Candida species (Figure 2e). However, no significant inflammatory cell infiltration was associated with these mycotic hyperkeratotic foci with only occasional mononuclear inflammatory cells evidenced.

The multinodular mesenteric mass histologically consisted of extensive necrotic fat demonstrating ‘fat saponification’, cholesterol clefts, mineralisation, granulomatous inflammation and marked fibrosis. The histologic diagnosis made was marked mesenteric fat necrosis with extensive chronic active granulomatous steatitis. However, it is considered likely that the mass reflects necrosis of a previously strangulated lipoma.

Other histologic findings included marked pulmonary congestion with oedema, mineralised pulmonary micro‐thrombosis, parathyroid hyperplasia, marked testicular hypospermatogenesis, lymph node atrophy and minimal to mild regional intestinal congestion and enteritis.

sec.title: DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: The kidneys exhibited features of chronic end‐stage renal disease that is consistent with the observed uremia. While chronic nephritis was present, extensive acute RPN (medullary crest necrosis or necrotising pyelitis) was also present associated with intra‐lesional Candida organisms. Both Candida albicans and Escherichia coli were isolated from the kidney. Polycystic renal disease was also present.

The findings collectively suggest the superimposition of acute RPN onto chronic nephritis; that in turn facilitated an opportunistic renal infection arising from oral candidiasis (Aiello, 2016; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005; Jones et al., 1997; Tomashefski & Abramowsky, 1981). Conversely, RPN may have been initiated by occupation of the papilla with the mycotic organisms as described for systemic Candida infections in animals (Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016) and humans (Bach & Thanh, 1998; Brix, 2002; Fisher, 2011; Gandhi et al., 2005; Tomashefski & Abramowsky, 1981).

Candida species occur in a variety of animal species including humans, generally present as commensals of the skin, digestive and urogenital tracts (AVMA Guidelines for the Euthanasia of Animals, 2013; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Fisher, 2011; Gandhi et al., 2005; Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005; Quinn et al., 2002). Candidiasis usually occurs as an opportunistic yeast infection most often associated with the ubiquitous Candida albicans species (Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Gandhi et al., 2005; Quinn et al., 2002). While normally present as budding yeast forms along mucosal or skin surfaces, under appropriate conditions the organism may exhibit polymorphism with pathogenic tissue invasion and the variable occurrence of yeast, pseudohyphae and hyphae forms (Spellberg et al., 2005). The organism most often produces asymptomatic or mild superficial infections of the oral cavity or genitourinary tract, but occasionally results in systemic infections involving the kidney or other organs (Aiello, 2016; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Fisher, 2011; Gandhi et al., 2005; Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005; Quinn et al., 2002; Tomashefski & Abramowsky, 1981). Dissemination by embolisation from primary sites of local invasion can result in foci of necrosis with a mainly neutrophilic infiltrates containing masses of organisms occurring in the kidney (Barnes et al., 1983; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Fisher, 2011; Spellberg et al., 2005).

Candidiasis may occur as a complication of broad‐spectrum antibiotics, anti‐inflammatory agents, antineoplastics, immunosuppression, diabetes mellitus, prolonged glucocorticoid therapy, mechanical damage to mucus membranes and numerous other conditions (Aiello, 2016; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Fisher, 2011; Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005; Jones et al., 1997; Quinn et al., 2002). Studies on the pathogenesis of Candida infections suggest that lower urinary tract involvement typically results from retrograde infections, while renal parenchymal infections usually are associated with systemic infections or candidemia (antegrade infection) (Fisher, 2011; Gandhi et al., 2005; Spellberg et al., 2005).

The microscopic lesions in both the oral mucosa and kidney of our reported tiger demonstrated features generally considered characteristic for candidiasis (Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005). The oral microscopic changes were mild consisting of numerous small foci of parakeratotic hyperkeratosis associated with intra‐lesional Candida organisms (Figure 1d,e). The oral lesions did not contain neutrophils and generally appeared less dramatic than usually described for mucocutaneous candidiasis in dogs and cats (Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005), but were like lesions seen with more chronic forms of gingivitis or ‘catarrhal stomatitis’ (Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005). The renal lesions were similar to those reported for the systemic form of candidiasis (Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016).

While histological candidiasis was disclosed in the oral cavity and kidney, no microscopic evidence of Candida was apparent in the urinary bladder. Thus, the findings argue for a haematogenous (or antegrade) renal infection arising from the oral cavity population. The renal infection was possibly facilitated by the presence of papillary necrosis, polycystic renal disease, medications, dehydration and age‐associated or other causes of immunosuppression.

Little information is available in the scientific literature on the occurrence of renal disease in exotic cats (McCullagh & Lucke, 1978; Newkirk et al., 2011; Wolf et al., 1991). However, candidiasis in domestic felines may be associated with a variety of infections including those of the oral cavity, upper respiratory tracts, urinary bladder, intestine and eye (Aiello, 2016; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Ginn et al., 2007; Gross et al., 2005). While there are a few reports of the occurrence of RPN in tigers (Newkirk et al., 2011; Quinn et al., 2002), to our knowledge the presence of renal candidiasis associated with RPN has not been reported for this species. However, there are several descriptions of the association of renal candidiasis with papillary necrosis in humans and other animals (Bach & Thanh, 1998; Brix, 2002; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Tomashefski & Abramowsky, 1981; Wolf et al., 1991).

Causes of ‘renal medullary necrosis’ can generally be divided into those that are toxic or hypoxic. Examples include ‘analgesic nephropathy’ such as associated with non‐steroidal anti‐inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), dehydration and those resulting from compression of medullary vessels (such as urinary obstruction, pyelonephritis and amyloidosis; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016). Causes of RPN in animals include phenylbutazone therapy in horses and NSAID toxicosis in dogs and cats (Bach & Thanh, 1998; Brix, 2002; Khan & Mclean, 2012; Newkirk et al., 2011; Wolf et al., 1991). In humans, RPN may be associated with diabetes mellitus, analgesic abuse, chronic pyelonephritis, urinary tract obstruction, sickle cell haemoglobinopathy and renal transplants (Bach & Thanh, 1998; Brix, 2002; Wolf et al., 1991). RPN in humans is particularly common with analgesic drug abuse, and especially with NSAIDs (Brix, 2002). However, numerous other therapeutic agents or chemicals have been reported to potentially cause RPN in both animals and humans (Bach & Thanh, 1998; Brix, 2002).

McCullagh and Lucke (1978) reported the occurrence of RPN in two tigers consisting of large zones of liquefactive necrosis associated with minimal inflammation, but with extensive scarring in the cortex. Those authors suggested that the RPN may have been due to reduction in renal perfusion due to fibrosis and dehydration. Wolf et al. (1991) described the occurrence of RPN in two domestic cats and a tiger, emphasising that the occurrence of papillary necrosis in the absence of amyloidosis had not been reported for domestic or exotic felines. In their report, one of the domestic cats and the tiger had received a single treatment with a NSAID (Wolf et al., 1991).

A comprehensive review of renal lesions found during necropsy of 70 non‐domestic exotic felids at a municipal zoo was performed by Newkirk et al. (2011). The most common histologic lesion observed in the tiger cohort was lymphocytic interstitial nephritis being present in 9 of 25 (36%) of the tigers (Newkirk et al., 2011). RPN was observed in 13 of 70 (19%) of the total population, which included 6 of 25 (24%) of the tigers. They reported that for all cats RPN was significantly associated with historical NSAID treatment (odds ratio: 7.1; 95% confidence interval, 1.9–26.8). One tiger had RPN without concurrent nephritis.

Among the medications used in the currently reported tiger, one deserves consideration as possibly contributing to the development of RPN. Meloxicam is an NSAID often used for its an antipyretic, anti‐inflammation and analgesic properties (Khan & Mclean, 2012). Other possible contributory factors for RPN in this tiger include the presence of chronic kidney disease and dehydration (Brix, 2002; Clanciolo & Mohr, 2016; Corpa et al., 2005). Analgesic nephrotoxicity of NSAIDs may be enhanced by underlying kidney disease and dehydration; both of which were present in this reported tiger (Brix, 2002; Corpa et al., 2005). Experimental studies using sub‐lethal intravenous injections of Candida into mice produced partial necrosis of the tips of the renal pyramids associated with the presence of adherent fungal masses in the pelvis (Spellberg et al., 2005).

An association between RPN and renal candidiasis also occurs in humans (Tomashefski & Abramowsky, 1981). One study involving 42 autopsy patients manifesting with visceral candidiasis demonstrated that papillary necrosis occurred in nine patients (21%) in association with fungal invasion of the kidney (Tomashefski & Abramowsky, 1981).

The tiger subject to our report also demonstrated other pathology including the presence of polycystic kidney disease. Polycystic kidneys associated with hepatic cysts were reported for a 19‐year‐old male lion (Panthera leo) (Gerhauser et al., 2009). Inherited polycystic renal disease also occurs in certain domestic cat breeds (Bonazzi et al., 2007). However, the relationship (if any) between the occurrence of polycystic disease and the presence of candidiasis, papillary necrosis and chronic pyelonephritis in the tiger in this report is not known.

With respect to other pathology observed, the exact nature of the mesenteric fat necrosis is unclear. However, a relationship of the fat necrosis to the occurrence of RPN is unlikely. The mesenteric fat necrosis possibly reflects an underlying strangulated lipoma or other form of a pre‐existent mesenteric lipoma. A variety of apparent age‐associated changes were also appreciated that included evidence of lymph node atrophy and testicular atrophy with hypospermatogenesis.

In conclusion, oral and renal Candidiasis associated with terminal uremia was diagnosed in an aged tiger. The kidneys exhibited chronic nephritis and acute RPN. Candidiasis and associated papillary necrosis may have been predisposed by the chronic kidney disease, age‐associated immunosuppression or NSAID (meloxicam) therapy.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Floyd D. Wilson: Conceptualisation (lead); writing— original draft (lead); formal analysis (lead); writing—review and editing (equal). Lanny Pace: Writing—review and editing (equal); conceptualisation (supportive). Brittany Baughman: Writing—review and editing (equal); conceptualisation (supportive). Michael J. Mauel: Methodology (supportive); review and editing (supportive). Michael Holifield: Review and editing (equal). Donna Todd: Review and editing (equal).

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors confirm that the ethical policies of the journal, as noted on the journal's author guidelines page, have been adhered to.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1421.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: We greatly appreciate the help and cooperation of the Jackson Zoo in the preparation of this manuscript.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: No.
